root_cause: pool-svc was configured with slow database queries enabled and a database pool size of 5 while the load generator ran 12 concurrent /order workers. Each order held a pool connection in SELECT pg_sleep, exhausting the pool so health checks and user orders timed out waiting for a connection even though the service and Postgres were running.
actions_taken: Inspected container status, pool-svc and Postgres logs, service environment, compose configuration, HTTP health behavior, and pg_stat_activity. Confirmed five active SELECT pg_sleep sessions from pool-svc were occupying all pool connections. Updated only pool-svc in docker-compose.yaml to set SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1, recreated only the pool-svc container with docker compose up -d pool-svc, and verified /healthz and /order returned HTTP 200 repeatedly with zero PgSleep sessions remaining.
postmortem: A configuration intended to exercise slow queries allowed synthetic order load to monopolize every database connection in the small service pool. The health endpoint shared that same constrained pool, so it failed during saturation and made the service unavailable. This was a configuration and capacity isolation issue, not an individual mistake. Follow-ups should include guarding production configs against slow-query test settings, making health checks independent of the saturated order pool or reserving capacity for them, and adding alerts on pool acquisition latency and pool exhaustion before user-visible timeouts occur.
